subtopic_detail: Neonatal hypoglycaemia screening in infant of diabetic mother
stem: A term male infant has just been born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks to a mother with insulin-treated gestational diabetes who was on a basal-bolus regimen requiring 76 units per day at term. Birth weight is 4380 g (above 90th centile). Apgars are 9 and 9 and the baby has fed briefly at the breast within 30 minutes of birth and is now asleep in skin-to-skin contact with mother. He is pink, well perfused, and asymptomatic.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98% in air, axillary T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Pink, alert when handled, normal tone, no jitteriness, no cyanosis, no respiratory distress
data_table.Investigations: Nil performed since delivery 35 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in this infant's management?

options.text: Check a true blood glucose at 2 hours of age after the second feed, before further feeding, and act on a threshold of 2.6 mmol/L
options.rationale: Australian NHMRC and Queensland Clinical Guideline schedules for IDM and large-for-gestational-age neonates screen at 2 hours after a feed (not earlier, as transient nadir at 1 hour is physiological), then before subsequent feeds with an operational threshold of 2.6 mmol/L. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Defer all glucose testing until 6 hours of age provided the baby remains asymptomatic and feeds three-hourly with no jitteriness
options.rationale: IDM and LGA neonates require glucose screening from 2 hours regardless of symptoms because severe asymptomatic hypoglycaemia is well documented in this population during the first 12 hours. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Check a true blood glucose immediately and again at 4 hours, treating any reading below 3.3 mmol/L with intravenous dextrose
options.rationale: Immediate screening captures a physiological postnatal nadir and the 3.3 mmol/L threshold is not the Australian operational cut-off; routine intravenous dextrose without enteral feed trial is excessive in an asymptomatic feeding IDM. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Give 200 mg/kg of 40 percent buccal dextrose gel routinely at one hour of age regardless of the screening glucose value
options.rationale: Buccal dextrose gel is indicated when the screening glucose is below 2.6 mmol/L in an asymptomatic infant alongside a feed, not as universal prophylaxis irrespective of measured glucose. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Admit to special care nursery now for prophylactic 10 percent dextrose infusion at 60 mL/kg/day without further bedside glucose testing
options.rationale: Prophylactic intravenous dextrose is reserved for symptomatic infants or those failing to respond to enteral and buccal dextrose strategies; routine admission separates mother and baby unnecessarily in an asymptomatic IDM. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Newborn Hypoglycaemia)

explanation.summary: IDMs, LGA, SGA and late-preterm infants are routinely screened for hypoglycaemia from 2 hours of age after a feed. The operational threshold in Australia is 2.6 mmol/L; below this in an asymptomatic infant, treat with feed plus 200 mg/kg buccal 40 percent dextrose gel and recheck in 30 minutes. Symptomatic hypoglycaemia or persistent low readings escalate to IV dextrose. Earlier glucose checks pick up physiological nadir at 1 hour and prompt unnecessary escalation.
key_points: Risk groups: IDM, LGA, SGA, late preterm, perinatal stress.
Screen at 2 h after first feed, then pre-feed during 12 to 24 h.
Threshold 2.6 mmol/L; gel 200 mg/kg + feed for asymptomatic.
IV 10 percent dextrose if symptomatic or refractory.
explanation.pearls: The 2.6 mmol/L threshold and 2-hour-after-feed timing are the most commonly missed numbers; buccal dextrose gel 200 mg/kg is given with a feed, never instead of it.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline: Hypoglycaemia in Neonates

subtopic_detail: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis diagnostic pathway
stem: A 5-week-old first-born boy presents with three days of progressively forceful non-bilious vomiting that now occurs after every breastfeed and reaches the foot of the cot. He remains hungry and feeds eagerly after vomiting. He has lost 200 g since birth and last wet his nappy 8 hours ago. His mother had similar surgery as an infant. On examination he is alert with sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, and you palpate a firm mobile mass in the right upper quadrant during a test feed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 38, SpO2 99% air, T 36.7, weight 3.9 kg
data_table.Examination: Sunken fontanelle, CRT 3s, no abdominal distension, visible left-to-right gastric peristalsis after test feed, palpable olive RUQ
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.52, HCO3 33 mmol/L, Cl 88 mmol/L, K 3.1 mmol/L, Na 134 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound measuring pyloric muscle thickness and channel length against accepted paediatric thresholds
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the first-line confirmatory imaging in Australia; diagnostic thresholds are pyloric muscle thickness 3 mm or greater and channel length 15 mm or greater, with sensitivity above 95 percent and no radiation. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Upper gastrointestinal contrast study with barium swallow looking for a string sign at the pylorus
options.rationale: Contrast studies have been superseded by ultrasound in Australian paediatric centres because of radiation exposure and aspiration risk in a vomiting hypovolaemic infant; reserved for atypical presentations after a non-diagnostic ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy under general anaesthesia to visualise the pyloric channel and exclude mucosal pathology
options.rationale: Endoscopy is not indicated in infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis; it carries anaesthetic risk in a metabolically deranged infant and ultrasound is non-invasive and definitive. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Plain abdominal radiograph in supine and erect projections to identify the caterpillar sign and dilated stomach
options.rationale: Plain films are non-specific and neither confirm nor exclude pyloric stenosis; relying on them delays surgical referral and exposes the infant to ionising radiation unnecessarily. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Repeat test feed observation over 24 hours of inpatient admission with serial weight checks before any imaging
options.rationale: The presentation is already classic and biochemistry shows the typical hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis; delaying imaging prolongs dehydration and surgical correction. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis classically presents at 3 to 8 weeks with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting in a hungry feeder, palpable olive, and hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Ultrasound is the first-line confirmatory test in Australia using thresholds of muscle thickness at least 3 mm and channel length at least 15 mm. Management is resuscitation with 0.9 percent saline plus potassium to correct the alkalosis before Ramstedt pyloromyotomy. Family history (especially affected parent) increases risk substantially.
key_points: Classic 3-8 week age, hungry vomiter, projectile non-bilious.
Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is biochemical hallmark.
Ultrasound thresholds: muscle 3+ mm, channel 15+ mm.
Resuscitate alkalosis before surgery; not a surgical emergency.
explanation.pearls: Surgical referral waits for the bicarbonate to fall below 30 mmol/L and chloride above 100 mmol/L; operating on uncorrected alkalosis predisposes to post-operative apnoea.

sources.label: Royal Australasian College of Surgeons paediatric resources

stem:
A 9-month-old previously well girl presents with 14 hours of episodic inconsolable screaming and drawing knees up, occurring every 20 minutes between which she is pale and listless. She vomited twice (one episode bile-stained) and passed dark red jelly-like stool in the nappy two hours ago. She is hydrated and alert between episodes, with a tender sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant.

There is no peritonism and no distension. Ultrasound performed in the emergency department shows a target sign in the right upper quadrant consistent with ileocolic intussusception.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 144, RR 28, SpO2 99% air, BP 92/58, T 36.9, weight 8.5 kg
data_table.Examination: Alert between paroxysms, soft non-distended abdomen, palpable RUQ mass, no peritonism, redcurrant jelly stool on PR
data_table.Investigations: USS: target sign RUQ, no free fluid, normal blood flow on Doppler. FBC normal, electrolytes normal, lactate 1.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Empiric intravenous ceftriaxone and metronidazole then re-evaluate in 4 hours to see whether the paroxysms settle
options.rationale: Antibiotics alone do not reduce an intussusception; perforation has not occurred and broad-spectrum cover is not the initial intervention in a confirmed ileocolic intussusception without sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Immediate laparotomy with manual reduction in theatre without any prior attempt at radiological reduction in the department
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed pneumatic reduction, perforation, peritonism, or shock; primary operation in a stable child denies an 80 to 90 percent chance of avoiding laparotomy. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Barium contrast enema reduction in the radiology department with the family in the room throughout the procedure
options.rationale: Air contrast has replaced barium in Australian paediatric practice because air carries lower peritoneal contamination risk on perforation and higher reduction pressures are achievable safely. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Bowel rest with nasogastric decompression and intravenous fluids for 24 hours awaiting spontaneous resolution before reimaging
options.rationale: Spontaneous reduction is uncommon and delay risks ischaemia, perforation, and increased need for bowel resection; non-operative reduction should not be delayed in a confirmed case. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Pneumatic (air contrast) reduction under fluoroscopic guidance with paediatric surgical team scrubbed for immediate laparotomy
options.rationale: Pneumatic (air contrast) reduction is the first-line definitive intervention in haemodynamically stable ileocolic intussusception without perforation or peritonism, with success rates of 80 to 90 percent and surgical backup mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception in the 3 month to 3 year age range is managed by pneumatic reduction under fluoroscopy as the first-line definitive intervention when the child is haemodynamically stable, without peritonism, and without free air. Surgical team must be available for immediate laparotomy in case of perforation or failed reduction. Recurrence after pneumatic reduction occurs in approximately 10 percent and these are usually managed by repeat reduction. A pathological lead point (Meckel diverticulum, lymphoma, polyp) should be considered in children over 3 years or with recurrent episodes.
key_points: Pneumatic reduction is first-line for stable cases.
Contraindications: perforation, peritonism, shock.
Surgical backup essential during reduction.
Consider lead point in >3 y or recurrent cases.
explanation.pearls: Maximum air pressure for reduction is 120 mmHg under fluoroscopy; perforation rate is under 1 percent, and post-reduction the child stays for 24 hours observation.

subtopic_detail: Cystic fibrosis newborn screening positive workup
stem: A 4-week-old boy is referred by the state newborn bloodspot screening program. His day-3 immunoreactive trypsinogen was 122 micrograms per litre (well above the 99th centile cutoff of 99). Reflex molecular testing on the same bloodspot identified a single F508del CFTR variant. He was born at term, fed well from birth, and his parents are both healthy with no family history of cystic fibrosis. Examination today is unremarkable with appropriate weight gain.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 38, SpO2 99% air, T 36.7, weight 4.2 kg (50th centile)
data_table.Examination: Pink, well perfused, no respiratory distress, no nasal polyps, soft abdomen, normal genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Newborn screen: IRT 122 micrograms/L (cutoff 99), single F508del heterozygote on reflex panel
data_table.Medications: Vitamin K at birth, breastfed exclusively

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in this infant's diagnostic workup?

options.text: Start oral pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy and fat-soluble vitamin supplementation empirically while awaiting further testing
options.rationale: Pancreatic insufficiency is treated once confirmed by faecal elastase or weight faltering after diagnosis; empiric enzyme therapy in an asymptomatic infant pre-diagnosis is inappropriate. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia: Diagnosis Guidelines)

options.text: Full extended CFTR gene sequencing performed immediately, deferring all sweat chloride testing until the molecular results return
options.rationale: Sequencing is the second-tier test when sweat chloride is intermediate or when a single variant complicates classification; it is not the immediate next test ahead of sweat chloride. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia: Diagnosis Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the parents that single-variant heterozygotes are healthy carriers and discharge without further testing
options.rationale: Australian newborn screening programs refer all infants with raised IRT and one or more CFTR variants for sweat testing because the bloodspot panel does not exhaustively detect every CF-causing variant. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia: Diagnosis Guidelines)

options.text: High-resolution chest computed tomography to assess for early bronchiectasis before any diagnostic biochemistry
options.rationale: Radiological imaging is not part of the diagnostic pathway in an asymptomatic newborn screen positive infant and carries radiation exposure with no diagnostic yield at this stage. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia: Diagnosis Guidelines)

options.text: Sweat chloride testing at a specialist cystic fibrosis centre using pilocarpine iontophoresis with paired bilateral collections
options.rationale: The gold standard confirmatory test after positive newborn screening is sweat chloride by pilocarpine iontophoresis at an accredited paediatric CF centre, with chloride 60 mmol/L or greater diagnostic, 30 to 59 intermediate, and under 30 normal in infants. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia: Diagnosis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian newborn screening uses raised IRT plus reflex CFTR variant detection; any infant with positive bloodspot screen is referred for sweat chloride testing at an accredited CF centre at 4 to 6 weeks of age. Sweat chloride 60 mmol/L or greater confirms CF, 30 to 59 mmol/L is intermediate (CRMS/CFSPID, needing ongoing follow-up), and under 30 mmol/L excludes CF. Diagnosis triggers entry to a multidisciplinary paediatric CF service for surveillance, enzyme assessment, airway clearance education, and CFTR-modulator eligibility consideration.
key_points: Sweat chloride cutoffs: <30 normal, 30-59 intermediate, >=60 CF.
Test by pilocarpine iontophoresis at accredited centre.
Single variant after screen needs sweat test, not reassurance.
Multidisciplinary CF service for confirmed diagnoses.
explanation.pearls: Sweat chloride should be paired (both arms) with at least 75 mg of sweat collected per arm; insufficient sweat is the commonest cause of an unsatisfactory test and warrants repeat at 4 to 6 weeks of age.

subtopic_detail: Ectopic pregnancy diagnostic threshold hCG and transvaginal ultrasound
stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 with one previous tubal ectopic 18 months ago, treated medically with methotrexate, presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at 6 weeks 4 days by LMP with 2 days of left iliac fossa pain and minimal dark vaginal bleeding. She is haemodynamically stable. Quantitative serum beta-hCG today is 2840 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with a thin endometrium, no gestational sac, no pseudosac, and a 14 mm complex adnexal mass adjacent to the left ovary with a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Both ovaries are visualised separately.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft with left iliac fossa tenderness, no rebound, cervical motion tenderness on bimanual, no adnexal mass clinically
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG 2840 IU/L; Hb 124 g/L; group A positive. TVUS: empty uterus, left adnexal complex mass 14 mm separate from ovary, small free fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with safety-netting and review in 7 days to repeat serum beta-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound assessment
options.rationale: Ambulatory expectant management is reserved for women with low and falling hCG, no visualised ectopic, and minimal symptoms; this woman has a visualised mass, prior ectopic and ongoing pain. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Schedule diagnostic laparoscopy in the next available elective list within two weeks for definitive visualisation
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already secure on transvaginal imaging plus hCG; deferring intervention by weeks risks rupture, and elective scheduling is not appropriate for confirmed ectopic. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Repeat serum beta-hCG in 48 hours and re-image only if values rise less than expected before considering intervention
options.rationale: The discriminatory zone is exceeded (>1500 IU/L by TVUS) with no intrauterine pregnancy and a visualised extrauterine mass in a high-risk woman; delay risks tubal rupture. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre immediately without further imaging or assessment of suitability
options.rationale: Methotrexate eligibility requires confirmation of haemodynamic stability, hCG criteria, size, no fetal heart, no rupture, ability to comply with follow-up, and exclusion of contraindications; it is not a default first move. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Diagnose ectopic pregnancy and admit for treatment selection based on size, hCG level, symptoms, and patient preference
options.rationale: A beta-hCG above the transvaginal discriminatory zone of 1500 IU/L with an empty uterus and visualised adnexal mass meets criteria for ectopic pregnancy; admission for staged decision on medical (methotrexate) versus surgical (laparoscopic salpingectomy or salpingostomy) management is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Statement)

explanation.summary: Diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy in Australia is made on transvaginal ultrasound visualising an extrauterine sac or adnexal mass together with a serum beta-hCG above the transvaginal discriminatory zone of 1500 IU/L and an empty uterus. Once diagnosed, choice between expectant, medical (methotrexate), and surgical (laparoscopic salpingectomy preferred, salpingostomy if contralateral tube damaged) management depends on hCG level, size, presence of fetal heart, symptoms, and patient preference. Anti-D prophylaxis is given to RhD-negative women.
key_points: Discriminatory zone TVUS: 1500 IU/L (TAUS 2000).
Empty uterus + adnexal mass + hCG > 1500 = ectopic.
Methotrexate criteria: stable, hCG <5000, <35mm, no FH, compliant.
Anti-D 250 IU IM if RhD negative.
explanation.pearls: Methotrexate dose is 50 mg/m^2 IM with day-4 and day-7 hCG; if hCG falls under 15 percent between day 4 and 7, repeat dose; suboptimal fall requires surgery.

sources.label: AFP: Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Vaginal discharge differential and bacterial vaginosis treatment
stem: A 27-year-old woman presents to general practice with a 1-week history of malodorous greyish vaginal discharge worse after intercourse. She denies vulval itch, dysuria, or pelvic pain. She has a regular male partner of 18 months, uses combined oral contraception, and is up to date with cervical screening (HPV negative 6 months ago). Speculum examination shows thin homogenous grey discharge coating the vaginal walls and a normal-appearing cervix. Vaginal pH on litmus is 5.2. The whiff test with 10 percent potassium hydroxide produces a strong fishy odour.

data_table.Examination: Vulva normal, vagina with thin grey homogenous discharge coating walls, no erythema, cervix normal, bimanual non-tender, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.2; whiff test positive; saline microscopy shows clue cells with absence of lactobacilli

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Oral metronidazole 2 g as a single dose for both the patient and her regular male partner taken simultaneously
options.rationale: Routine partner treatment is not recommended in BV because it does not reduce recurrence; the 2 g single dose also has higher recurrence than the 7-day regimen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Vaginitis)

options.text: Empirical oral azithromycin 1 g single dose plus ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly for presumed chlamydia and gonorrhoea
options.rationale: She is asymptomatic for STI, recently screened, and has Amsel-positive BV; empiric STI cover is not indicated and does not treat BV. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Vaginitis)

options.text: Reassurance that the symptoms reflect physiological discharge variation with watchful waiting for 6 weeks before any treatment
options.rationale: Confirmed BV in a symptomatic woman warrants treatment to reduce symptoms and to lower risk of post-procedural infection if any uterine instrumentation is needed; watchful waiting is inappropriate when diagnostic criteria are met. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Vaginitis)

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days with abstinence from alcohol during and 24 hours after treatment
options.rationale: Amsel criteria (pH above 4.5, clue cells, positive whiff test, thin homogenous discharge) confirm BV; first-line in Australia is oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days. Single 2 g dose has higher recurrence and is second line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Vaginitis)

options.text: Oral fluconazole 150 mg as a single dose with planned telephone review in two weeks for confirmation of symptom resolution
options.rationale: Fluconazole treats candidiasis, which presents with itch, white curdy discharge, normal pH below 4.5, and pseudohyphae on microscopy; her findings (pH 5.2, whiff positive, clue cells) confirm bacterial vaginosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Vaginitis)

explanation.summary: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed by Amsel criteria (3 of 4: pH above 4.5, positive whiff with KOH, clue cells on saline microscopy, thin homogenous grey discharge) or Nugent score on Gram stain. First-line treatment in Australia is oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days. Topical metronidazole gel or clindamycin cream are equivalent alternatives in non-pregnant women. Routine partner treatment is not recommended. Recurrence is common; in recurrent BV, suppressive metronidazole vaginal gel twice weekly for 4 to 6 months is used.
key_points: Amsel: pH>4.5, whiff+, clue cells, thin grey discharge.
First-line: oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 7 days.
Avoid 2 g single dose (higher recurrence).
No routine partner treatment for BV.
explanation.pearls: Counsel disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol during metronidazole and for 24 hours after; pregnant women with symptomatic BV are treated similarly because BV increases preterm birth risk.

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 at 38 weeks 4 days with a transverse-lying second twin (cephalic first twin delivered uneventfully 12 minutes ago) is being prepared for vaginal delivery of the second twin in the birth suite. Her membranes have just ruptured spontaneously with a large gush of clear fluid. The midwife calls you urgently because the cardiotocograph shows a sudden prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm with no recovery for 90 seconds, and she has palpated a pulsating loop of umbilical cord protruding from the introitus. The woman is in lithotomy position with an epidural in situ. Theatre is two floors away.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 92, BP 122/76, SpO2 99%; fetal HR 70 bpm prolonged deceleration
data_table.Examination: Visible pulsating cord at introitus, presenting part high and transverse, cervix fully dilated, epidural functioning
data_table.Investigations: Earlier USS at admission: twin 2 transverse, EFW 2.9 kg, posterior placenta, normal AFI

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Knee-chest position with manual elevation of the presenting part and transfer to theatre for category-1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Knee-chest plus transfer is the standard manoeuvre for cord prolapse with the cervix not fully dilated; with full dilatation and an operator able to deliver the second twin, vaginal delivery is faster and avoids the time cost of theatre transfer. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance)

options.text: Internal podalic version and breech extraction of the second twin by the most senior accoucheur available in the room
options.rationale: With a fully dilated cervix, an experienced operator, functioning epidural, and immediate threat to the fetus, internal podalic version and breech extraction is faster than transfer to theatre for caesarean and is the recommended management for cord prolapse with the second twin under these conditions. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance)

options.text: Resume continuous fetal monitoring while encouraging maternal pushing for two more contractions before reassessing presentation
options.rationale: The fetus is in prolonged bradycardia from cord compression and the second twin is transverse: pushing will not deliver a transverse fetus, and delay risks fetal demise. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance)

options.text: Replace the cord into the vagina and apply warm saline-soaked gauze while awaiting senior obstetric review in the next 15 minutes
options.rationale: Cord replacement is not recommended (high risk of further compression and vasospasm), and waiting 15 minutes risks hypoxic injury; immediate delivery is required. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous terbutaline 250 micrograms and bladder filling with 500 mL warm saline while planning vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Tocolysis and bladder filling are adjuncts during transfer to theatre when the cervix is not fully dilated; with full dilatation and a deliverable second twin, immediate operative vaginal delivery or internal version is preferred. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fetal Surveillance)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with a fully dilated cervix is delivered immediately by the fastest route. For a cephalic singleton at full dilatation, instrumental vaginal delivery is the fastest. For a transverse second twin at full dilatation with an experienced operator, internal podalic version and breech extraction is the standard. If the cervix is not fully dilated, knee-chest position, manual elevation of the presenting part, bladder filling with warm saline and tocolysis with terbutaline are used to buy time for transfer to theatre for category-1 caesarean. Cord replacement is not recommended.
key_points: Full dilatation + experienced operator = fastest vaginal route.
Transverse second twin: internal version + breech extraction.
Not fully dilated: knee-chest, elevate part, fill bladder, transfer.
Tocolysis (terbutaline 250 mcg SC) for transfer.
explanation.pearls: Bladder filling with 500 mL warm saline elevates the presenting part better than manual elevation during prolonged transfer; document time from prolapse to delivery as it predicts neonatal outcome.

subtopic_detail: Severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome inpatient management
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department 7 days after transvaginal oocyte retrieval for IVF (her first cycle for unexplained infertility). She has 4 days of progressive abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, reduced urine output, and shortness of breath when lying flat. She has gained 4 kg since the day of retrieval. The retrieval recovered 28 oocytes and embryo transfer was deferred 'for medical reasons'. She has no chest pain or unilateral leg swelling. Her partner is in the room.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112, BP 96/58, RR 22, SpO2 95% air, T 36.8, weight 74 kg (up from 70 kg)
data_table.Examination: Tense ascitic abdomen with shifting dullness, JVP not seen, reduced air entry both bases, no calf tenderness, urine output 18 mL/h on catheter
data_table.Investigations: Hb 168 g/L, Hct 0.50, WCC 18.4, platelets 412, sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 4.2, urea 11.4, creatinine 124, albumin 26 g/L, LFT mildly raised, CXR small bilateral pleural effusions, USS bilaterally enlarged ovaries 11 cm with multiple cysts, large ascites
data_table.Medications: Triggered with hCG 5000 units 8 days ago; nil regular medications

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous furosemide 40 mg twice daily to remove ascitic fluid and relieve respiratory compromise more rapidly
options.rationale: Diuretics in OHSS worsen intravascular depletion (the problem is third-spacing with low effective circulating volume) and risk acute kidney injury and venous thromboembolism; not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Statement)

options.text: Bilateral percutaneous ovarian aspiration in interventional radiology to decompress the enlarged ovaries and reduce VEGF release
options.rationale: Ovarian aspiration is contraindicated because of haemorrhage and torsion risk; OHSS treatment targets the fluid third-space (ascitic drainage) and supportive care, not the ovaries themselves. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Statement)

options.text: Admit for intravenous crystalloid resuscitation, prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily, and therapeutic paracentesis if tense ascites compromises breathing
options.rationale: Severe OHSS management combines volume resuscitation with crystalloid +/- albumin, thromboprophylaxis (LMWH for at least the haemoconcentration period), respiratory support with therapeutic paracentesis if tense ascites, and admission with strict monitoring; deferred embryo transfer is already in place to prevent worsening from endogenous hCG. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Statement)

options.text: Schedule urgent embryo transfer in the next 48 hours to avoid wasting the IVF cycle while supportive care continues
options.rationale: Pregnancy generates endogenous hCG which prolongs and worsens OHSS for weeks; fresh transfer should be cancelled with embryos cryopreserved for frozen transfer once recovered. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Statement)

options.text: Discharge home with oral fluid restriction to 1 litre per day and outpatient telephone review at 48 hours by the fertility unit
options.rationale: She meets severe OHSS criteria (haemoconcentration, oliguria, ascites with effusions, hyponatraemia, raised creatinine); discharge is unsafe and severe OHSS requires inpatient care. (Source: RANZCOG OHSS Statement)

explanation.summary: Severe OHSS results from VEGF-mediated capillary leak after hCG-triggered ovarian stimulation; risk peaks 7 to 10 days post-trigger and worsens markedly if pregnancy supervenes (endogenous hCG). Management is admission, careful crystalloid resuscitation, LMWH thromboprophylaxis (VTE risk is markedly elevated), paracentesis for tense ascites, freeze-all of embryos, and serial monitoring of haematocrit, electrolytes, and renal function. Diuretics and cabergoline have specific limited roles. Critical-care escalation is needed if ARDS or thromboembolism develops.
key_points: Severe OHSS: haemoconcentration, oliguria, ascites, effusions.
Risk peaks day 7-10 post-trigger; worsens with pregnancy.
Crystalloid + LMWH + paracentesis if tense.
Freeze all embryos in severe cases.
explanation.pearls: Risk factors for OHSS include young age, low BMI, PCOS, high antral follicle count, oocyte retrieval >20, and rising serum oestradiol above 11000 pmol/L on trigger day; GnRH agonist trigger with freeze-all virtually eliminates severe OHSS in high-risk women.

subtopic_detail: SSRI discontinuation syndrome recognition versus relapse
stem:
A 34-year-old woman returns to her GP 6 days after stopping paroxetine 20 mg daily, which she has taken for 14 months for a single episode of major depression now in stable remission. She stopped after running out of script while travelling and forgot to refill. She reports vivid 'electric shock' sensations in her head when she moves her eyes, dizziness when standing, nausea, vivid disturbing dreams, and feeling anxious and irritable.

Her mood is reactive, she is enjoying her usual activities, sleeps well after initial dreaming subsides, eats normally, and denies anhedonia, hopelessness, or suicidal thoughts.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented, full affect range, eye movements normal, no focal neurology, gait normal
data_table.Medications: Paroxetine 20 mg daily ceased 6 days ago; combined oral contraception

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch to fluoxetine 20 mg daily as a long half-life bridge then taper fluoxetine over 4 weeks instead of paroxetine
options.rationale: Fluoxetine bridging is reserved for severe persistent discontinuation symptoms despite reintroduction of the original SSRI; first step is to resume the original drug and taper. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Antidepressant Discontinuation)

options.text: Commence prochlorperazine 5 mg three times daily and ondansetron 8 mg three times daily for symptomatic relief of nausea and dizziness
options.rationale: Symptomatic treatment with antiemetics does not address the underlying serotonergic withdrawal and adds prochlorperazine-related extrapyramidal risk; addressing the cause is preferable. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Antidepressant Discontinuation)

options.text: Reassure that symptoms will resolve spontaneously within 7 days and recommend continued cessation without resuming any medication
options.rationale: Symptoms are functionally limiting and last 1 to 3 weeks if untreated with paroxetine (highest-risk SSRI for discontinuation due to short half-life); resumption and slow taper is recommended over watchful waiting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Antidepressant Discontinuation)

options.text: Diagnose major depressive recurrence and restart paroxetine 20 mg with referral for outpatient psychiatry assessment within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Mood remains in remission with reactive affect, preserved enjoyment, sleep, appetite, and no suicidality; symptom timing and physical features (paraesthesia, electric shocks) point to discontinuation syndrome rather than depressive recurrence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Antidepressant Discontinuation)

options.text: Restart paroxetine 20 mg daily and taper over 4 to 6 weeks once symptoms resolve, with close follow-up to monitor for recurrence
options.rationale: Onset within days of cessation with classic FINISH features (flu-like, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, sensory paraesthesia, hyperarousal) plus preserved mood and reactivity strongly favours discontinuation syndrome; resumption resolves symptoms within 24 to 48 hours then slow taper prevents recurrence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Antidepressant Discontinuation)

explanation.summary: SSRI discontinuation syndrome appears within 1 to 7 days of stopping or markedly reducing an SSRI/SNRI and is most common with paroxetine and venlafaxine (short half-lives). Features (FINISH mnemonic) include flu-like symptoms, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, sensory disturbance (electric-shock paraesthesia), and hyperarousal. Distinguish from depressive recurrence by rapid onset, prominent physical/sensory features, preserved mood reactivity, and resolution within 24 to 48 hours of reinstating the SSRI. Management is restart and slow taper, with fluoxetine bridging reserved for resistant cases.
key_points: Onset 1-7 days post stop; resolves within 1-3 weeks if untreated.
Paroxetine and venlafaxine highest risk.
FINISH: flu, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, sensory, hyperarousal.
Restart + slow taper; fluoxetine bridge only if persistent.
explanation.pearls: Taper paroxetine by 5 mg every 1 to 2 weeks (or slower) and watch for re-emergence at each step; agomelatine and bupropion have low discontinuation rates and may be preferable in patients with poor taper tolerance.

subtopic_detail: Borderline personality disorder acute self-harm crisis management
stem:
A 24-year-old woman with established borderline personality disorder under a community mental health team (currently in week 14 of an 18-month dialectical behaviour therapy program) presents to the emergency department after taking 14 paracetamol tablets at 9 pm following an argument with her boyfriend. It is now 11 pm. She immediately told her mother who brought her in. She has had three similar low-lethality overdoses in the past 18 months.

She is calm now, regrets the act, and says she 'just wanted to feel something stop'. She denies current suicidal intent and has agreed verbally to use her crisis card and call her DBT therapist tomorrow morning. Her therapist is contactable now if needed.

data_table.Examination: Alert, full conversation, no signs of intoxication, no fresh self-harm wounds, mood reactive, no psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol level pending (4 h sample at 1 am); ALT 22, INR 1.0, creatinine 68
data_table.Medications: Currently nil regular; trialled three SSRIs previously with no benefit

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management approach after treating the paracetamol overdose?

options.text: Treat the overdose, liaise with her DBT and crisis teams, activate her agreed safety plan, and discharge home with mother and 24-hour follow-up
options.rationale: NHMRC guidelines recommend avoiding lengthy psychiatric admission for BPD self-harm and instead coordinating with the existing structured treatment (DBT) team, activating an agreed safety plan, ensuring rapid follow-up and engaging family/carer support; this preserves therapeutic momentum and avoids reinforcing maladaptive coping. (Source: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Commence quetiapine 100 mg nocte as ongoing mood stabilisation and discharge with a 7-day pharmacy supply for self-administration
options.rationale: There is no PBS-recognised medication that treats BPD itself; quetiapine has unproven benefit, sedates, contributes to metabolic side effects, and a 7-day supply hands a vulnerable patient potential overdose means. (Source: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Request an inpatient Mental Health Act assessment for involuntary admission to enforce safety despite her stated agreement to use the crisis plan
options.rationale: Involuntary admission for chronic recurrent low-lethality self-harm in BPD is not therapeutic, breaches least-restrictive principles, and may damage the established therapeutic relationship; criteria for compulsion are not met. (Source: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Admit to an acute mental health inpatient unit for at least 7 days for observation, behavioural stabilisation, and medication review
options.rationale: Brief admission for BPD self-harm is associated with worsening behaviour, regression, and dependency; Australian guidelines advise the shortest possible admission only when there is acute risk that cannot be managed in the community. (Source: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Discharge without communication to the community mental health team and advise her to call Lifeline if distress returns at any point
options.rationale: Discharge without contacting the existing care team fragments care and misses the opportunity to align with her individualised crisis and safety plan; this is the commonest preventable adverse outcome after ED presentation in BPD. (Source: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: For self-harm crises in BPD, Australian NHMRC guidelines emphasise active coordination with the existing structured psychotherapy team (DBT, MBT, schema), use of the patient's individualised safety plan, family/carer involvement when agreed, and avoiding both lengthy inpatient admission (which can reinforce maladaptive behaviour) and reflexive involuntary detention. Brief admission is reserved for risk that cannot be managed in community. No psychotropic medication has been shown to treat the core features of BPD; pharmacotherapy is reserved for clearly diagnosed comorbid disorders and reviewed regularly. Rapid follow-up within 24 to 72 hours is critical.
key_points: Avoid prolonged admission for BPD self-harm.
Coordinate with existing DBT/MBT/schema team.
Activate individualised safety plan + carer involvement.
No drug treats BPD; treat clear comorbidities only.
explanation.pearls: Means restriction (small quantities of analgesics, locked supply with carer) reduces lethality of impulsive overdoses; document the safety plan jointly and share it with the GP and DBT team before discharge.

sources.label: NHMRC Borderline Personality Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline
RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for BPD
